creator_thesis: @didoriot applies marketing and cultural critique to internet trends, influencer aesthetics, and consumer behavior — treating brand decisions, platform dynamics, and online identity as legitimate objects of analysis. The creator operates from inside the culture they critique: a salaried professional who also writes on Substack, crosses between TikTok and Instagram, and is openly figuring out the creator economy in real time. The throughline is the tension between aspiration and reality — in careers, in consumption, in how people perform themselves online.

key_concepts.name: Goop-ification / conversation piece products
key_concepts.definition: Brands releasing products that function as cultural signals rather than functional goods — earnestly marketed as wellness, but really purchased to virtue-signal membership in an increasingly niche audience.

key_concepts.name: Parasocial management of the Instagram boyfriend
key_concepts.definition: Influencers strategically calibrate partner visibility to protect audience parasocial attachment; the only socially acceptable way to feature a boyfriend online is through complete gender role reversal — putting him behind the camera.

key_concepts.name: Attention span as future capital
key_concepts.definition: AI advancement will bifurcate the workforce into a 'thinking class' that can manage its own attention and cognition versus those who cannot — positioning attention span as the new EQ/IQ differentiator.

key_concepts.name: Gen Alpha marketing overreach
key_concepts.definition: Brands crossing unspoken ethical boundaries by marketing adult wellness products to children, coinciding with legislative responses like Australia's under-16 social media ban.

key_concepts.name: 'You can just do things' / free will discourse
key_concepts.definition: A Gen Z cultural meme about reclaiming agency as a response to decision paralysis caused by infinite online options — the generational successor to the millennial 'adulting' frame.

key_concepts.name: Dream job displacement and the Substack era
key_concepts.definition: Institutional cultural authority (Vogue, print media) has collapsed into the creator economy; the new aspirational 'cool girl' career is Substack newsletter writer — fashionable, smart, and immune to layoffs.

key_concepts.name: Gendered etiquette curriculum online
key_concepts.definition: Social media replicates centuries of formal femininity instruction by converting individual women (Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy, 'that girl') into gendered templates — diluting the original essence that made them compelling, and making girls lonelier.

key_concepts.name: Provocation marketing / controversy as free engagement
key_concepts.definition: Large multinationals deliberately court backlash (AI Super Bowl ads, boycotted brand Coachella trips) because hate comments are still engagement and negative press clarifies their core consumer against everyone else.

references.name: The Bell Jar (Sylvia Plath) — fig tree analogy
Real Housewives / Secret Lives of Mormon Wives

positions: "You can't follow a tutorial on how to seem like you've never followed a tutorial" — gendered self-optimization content destroys the very quality it promises to teach. (DWHfsAcCWqI)
"There's too much content online. So we buy these products to virtue signal to an increasingly niche audience who understands what these things are and what they say about us." (DRU2xzpiS4w)

"The only thing that's embarrassing is publicly needing or wanting a man" — having a boyfriend is still a status symbol; what's changed is that dependence has become the taboo, not the relationship itself. (DU1e1q7iVMb)
"The medium has changed, the message has not" — social media is the latest iteration of centuries-old gendered etiquette instruction, and it's making girls lonelier than ever. (DWHfsAcCWqI)

"Do what makes you unique and special because that cannot and will not be replicated by AI" — the collapse of junior corporate roles is also an opening to pursue genuine creative interests. (DUJDVG0jgcq)
